approaches.name: Brute Force Recursion O(2^n)
approaches.summary: Generate all subsequences recursively, check each for palindrome property, and store unique ones in a set. Use backtracking to explore all combinations, making it exhaustive but inefficient for large strings.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Different Palindromic Subsequences via Brute Force Recursion
ladders.desc: Complete implementation generating all subsequences, checking palindromes, and counting unique ones using backtracking.
ladders.explanation: This approach solves the problem by exhaustively exploring all possible subsequences of the input string. The intuition is that any palindromic subsequence must be formed by selecting characters from the original string while maintaining their relative order. We use recursion with backtracking to generate all 2^n possible subsequences (including the empty subsequence). For each generated subsequence, we check if it's a palindrome by comparing characters from both ends. Unique palindromic subsequences are stored in a set to avoid duplicates. The algorithm works by making two recursive choices at each character position: include the current character in the subsequence or skip it. When we reach the end of the string, we have a complete subsequence to check. The time complexity is O(2^n * n) because we generate 2^n subsequences and checking each palindrome takes O(n) time. Space complexity is O(2^n) for storing unique subsequences in the worst case. This approach connects to the parent problem by providing a complete, albeit inefficient, solution that guarantees correctness through exhaustive search. Common mistakes include forgetting to handle empty subsequences, not properly backtracking, and inefficient palindrome checking. The real pattern involves depth-first search with state tracking.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Generate All Subsequences via Backtracking
ladders.desc: Recursive function that explores include/skip choices for each character to produce all possible subsequences.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core generator that creates all 2^n subsequences of a string. The intuition is that for each character in the string, we have two choices: include it in the current subsequence or skip it. By recursively exploring all combinations of these choices, we can generate every possible subsequence. The algorithm uses depth-first search with backtracking: we maintain a current subsequence (as a string or list), and at each position i in the original string, we make two recursive calls—one including s[i] and one skipping it. When we reach the end of the string (i == n), we have a complete subsequence that can be processed. Time complexity is O(2^n) to generate all subsequences, and space complexity is O(n) for the recursion stack and current subsequence storage. This feeds into the parent problem by providing the complete set of subsequences that will later be checked for palindrome property. Common mistakes include modifying the current subsequence incorrectly (needing to backtrack by popping added characters) and not handling the base case properly. The pattern is classic combinatorial generation.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Start at index 0 with current subsequence ''
Step 2: At index 0 (character 'a'):
- Choice 1: Include 'a' → current becomes 'a', recurse to index 1
  At index 1 (character 'b'):
  - Include 'b' → current becomes 'ab', index 2 (end) → add 'ab' to results
  - Skip 'b' → current stays 'a', index 2 → add 'a' to results
- Choice 2: Skip 'a' → current stays '', recurse to index 1
  At index 1 (character 'b'):
  - Include 'b' → current becomes 'b', index 2 → add 'b' to results
  - Skip 'b' → current stays '', index 2 → add '' to results
Step 3: All subsequences generated: '', 'b', 'a', 'ab'

Step 1: Start at index 0 with current ''
Step 2: At index 0 (character 'a'):
- Include 'a' → current 'a', index 1 → add 'a'
- Skip 'a' → current '', index 1 → add ''
Step 3: Results: '', 'a'

ladders.title: Core Logic — Palindrome Checking for a String
ladders.desc: Algorithm to determine if a given string reads the same forwards and backwards.
ladders.explanation: This core logic verifies the palindrome property essential for the problem. The intuition is that a palindrome is symmetric around its center: the first character equals the last, the second equals the second-last, and so on. We can check this by comparing characters from both ends moving toward the center. The algorithm uses two pointers: one starting at the beginning (left) and one at the end (right). While left < right, compare s[left] with s[right]. If any pair doesn't match, the string is not a palindrome. If all pairs match (or we reach the center), it is a palindrome. Time complexity is O(n) where n is the string length, as we examine each character at most once. Space complexity is O(1) for the pointers. This feeds into level 1 by providing the verification step for each generated subsequence. Common mistakes include incorrect handling of empty strings (which are palindromes), off-by-one errors in pointer movement, and not considering case sensitivity (though the problem typically treats characters as given). The pattern is a straightforward two-pointer traversal.

examples.trace:
Step 1: left = 0 ('r'), right = 6 ('r') → match
Step 2: left = 1 ('a'), right = 5 ('a') → match
Step 3: left = 2 ('c'), right = 4 ('c') → match
Step 4: left = 3 ('e'), right = 3 → left >= right, stop
Step 5: All comparisons matched → palindrome
Step 1: left = 0 ('h'), right = 4 ('o') → 'h' != 'o' → not palindrome, stop immediately

ladders.title: Building Block — String Subsequence Concept
ladders.desc: Understanding what constitutes a subsequence and how it differs from substring.
ladders.explanation: This building block establishes the fundamental concept of a subsequence. A subsequence of a string is a sequence that can be derived by deleting zero or more characters without changing the order of the remaining characters. Unlike a substring, which must be contiguous, a subsequence allows gaps. For example, in "abc", "ac" is a subsequence (skipping 'b') but not a substring. The intuition is that we're selecting a subset of positions from the original string while preserving their relative order. The number of possible subsequences is 2^n (including the empty subsequence), as each character can be either included or excluded. This concept feeds into level 2 by defining what we're generating before checking for palindromes. Common mistakes include confusing subsequence with substring, forgetting the empty subsequence, or incorrectly counting duplicates when characters repeat. Understanding this is crucial for the brute-force approach because we need to generate all these possibilities.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Consider each character position (0,1,2)
Step 2: Generate by include/skip choices:
- Skip all: ""
- Include only position 0: "a"
- Include only position 1: "b"
- Include only position 2: "c"
- Include positions 0 and 1: "ab"
- Include positions 0 and 2: "ac"
- Include positions 1 and 2: "bc"
- Include all: "abc"
Step 3: Total 2^3 = 8 subsequences

Step 1: Positions 0 and 1 both have 'a'
Step 2: Generate:
- Skip both: ""
- Include only position 0: "a"
- Include only position 1: "a" (different position, same character)
- Include both: "aa"
Step 3: Note duplicates in value but distinct in formation

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Recursive Tree Traversal with State
ladders.desc: Implementing recursion that builds a solution incrementally and backtracks.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation covers the recursive pattern used to generate combinations. The intuition is modeling the problem as a decision tree: at each step (character position), we branch into two paths (include or skip), and we explore all paths to leaves (end of string). We maintain state—the current subsequence being built—and update it as we traverse. When we include a character, we append it to the current state; when we backtrack, we remove it to restore the previous state. The base case is when we've processed all characters, at which point we have a complete subsequence. Time complexity depends on the tree size (2^n branches), and space complexity is O(n) for the recursion depth and state. This feeds into level 3 by providing the mechanism to generate subsequences. Common mistakes include not copying state properly (leading to shared references), forgetting to backtrack, and stack overflow for deep recursion. The pattern is fundamental to combinatorial algorithms.

examples.input: Generate binary strings of length 2 via recursion
examples.trace:
Step 1: Start with empty string '' at position 0
Step 2: At position 0:
- Branch 1: Add '0' → current '0', recurse to position 1
  At position 1:
  - Add '0' → '00', position 2 → add to results
  - Add '1' → '01', position 2 → add to results
- Branch 2: Add '1' → current '1', recurse to position 1
  At position 1:
  - Add '0' → '10', position 2 → add to results
  - Add '1' → '11', position 2 → add to results
Step 3: All strings generated

examples.input: Generate all subsets of {1,2} via recursion
examples.trace:
Step 1: Start with empty list [] at index 0
Step 2: At index 0 (element 1):
- Include 1 → list [1], recurse to index 1
  At index 1 (element 2):
  - Include 2 → [1,2], index 2 → add to results
  - Skip 2 → [1], index 2 → add to results
- Skip 1 → list [], recurse to index 1
  At index 1 (element 2):
  - Include 2 → [2], index 2 → add to results
  - Skip 2 → [], index 2 → add to results
Step 3: All subsets generated

testCases.input: Generate all sequences of coin flips for 2 flips
Generate all ways to choose from {A,B} (include/skip)
Generate all combinations of 3 yes/no decisions
Generate all paths in a 2-step binary tree
Generate all strings by choosing a or b at each of 2 positions
Generate all number sequences of length 2 with digits 0-1

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — String Manipulation and Character Access
ladders.desc: Basic operations on strings: accessing characters by index and building strings incrementally.
ladders.explanation: This foundation covers the simplest prerequisites for working with strings in the algorithm. The intuition is that a string is a sequence of characters that can be accessed individually via indices (positions starting from 0). We can read characters, create new strings by concatenation, and check properties like length. For example, to build a subsequence, we start with an empty string and append characters one by one. To check a palindrome, we compare characters at symmetric indices. Time complexity for access is O(1), and for concatenation it can be O(k) where k is the string length. Space complexity depends on how strings are stored. This feeds into level 4 by providing the basic tools to manipulate the state in recursion. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in indices, confusing string immutability (needing to create new strings), and not handling empty strings properly. Understanding this is essential for any string-based algorithm.

examples.trace:
Step 1: String length = 5
Step 2: Access by index:
- s[0] returns 'h'
- s[1] returns 'e'
- s[2] returns 'l'
- s[3] returns 'l'
- s[4] returns 'o'
Step 3: Invalid index like s[5] would cause error

Step 1: Start with empty string ''
Step 2: Append 'c' → string becomes 'c'
Step 3: Append 'a' → string becomes 'ca'
Step 4: Append 't' → string becomes 'cat'
Step 5: Final string: "cat"

approaches.name: DP with Recursive Memoization O(n^2)
approaches.summary: Use recursion with memoization to count palindromic subsequences by considering start and end indices, handling cases where characters match or differ. Avoids recomputation by storing results in a memo table.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Different Palindromic Subsequences with DP Memoization
ladders.desc: Complete solution counting distinct palindromic subsequences using recursive DP with memoization.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires counting all distinct palindromic subsequences in a given string. A subsequence is a sequence derived by deleting some characters without changing order, and it's palindromic if it reads the same forwards and backwards. The key challenge is avoiding duplicate counts while efficiently exploring all possibilities. The DP with recursive memoization approach works by defining a recursive function count(i, j) that returns the number of distinct palindromic subsequences in the substring s[i:j+1]. The base cases are when i > j (empty substring, 0 palindromes) and i == j (single character, 1 palindrome). For the recursive case, if s[i] != s[j], then count(i, j) = count(i+1, j) + count(i, j-1) - count(i+1, j-1) because palindromes either include s[i], include s[j], or exclude both, with overlap subtracted. If s[i] == s[j], we need to carefully count palindromes that start and end with this matching character. We find the next occurrence of this character from the left (low) and right (high) within (i, j). If low > high, no inner occurrence exists, so count(i, j) = 2 * count(i+1, j-1) + 2 (for the single-character palindrome s[i] and the two-character palindrome s[i]s[j]). If low == high, exactly one inner occurrence exists, so count(i, j) = 2 * count(i+1, j-1) + 1 (adding the two-character palindrome). If low < high, multiple inner occurrences exist, so count(i, j) = 2 * count(i+1, j-1) - count(low+1, high-1) to avoid overcounting duplicates. We use memoization (a 2D array or dictionary) to store results of count(i, j) to avoid recomputation, ensuring O(n²) time and space. The intuition is that by breaking the problem into overlapping subproblems and handling matching characters carefully to prevent duplicates, we can efficiently compute the total. This connects to L1 as it implements the complete recursive logic with all cases. Common mistakes include not handling duplicate counts correctly when characters match, forgetting base cases, or using incorrect indices. The solution returns count(0, n-1) modulo 10^9+7 as typically required.

examples.trace: String: bccb, n=4. Compute count(0,3): s[0]=b, s[3]=b match. Find low: next b from left after index 0 within (0,3) is at index 3? Wait, need within (i,j) exclusive: indices 1 and 2. s[1]=c, s[2]=c, no b, so low > high (low=inf, high=-inf). Thus count(0,3) = 2 * count(1,2) + 2. Compute count(1,2): s[1]=c, s[2]=c match. Find low: next c from left after index 1 within (1,2) exclusive: no indices, so low > high. Thus count(1,2) = 2 * count(2,1) + 2. count(2,1): i=2, j=1, i>j, return 0. So count(1,2) = 2*0 + 2 = 2. Then count(0,3) = 2*2 + 2 = 6. Palindromic subsequences: b, c, cc, bcb, bccb, bb (but bb? Let's list: single: b, c; double: bb, cc, bc? Wait, bc not palindrome. Actually distinct palindromes: b, c, bb, cc, bcb, bccb. That's 6. Yes.

String: abcd, n=4. All characters distinct. Compute count(0,3): s[0]=a, s[3]=d, not equal. So count(0,3) = count(1,3) + count(0,2) - count(1,2). Compute count(1,3): s[1]=b, s[3]=d, not equal: count(1,3) = count(2,3) + count(1,2) - count(2,2). count(2,3): s[2]=c, s[3]=d, not equal: count(2,3) = count(3,3) + count(2,2) - count(3,2). count(3,3)=1, count(2,2)=1, count(3,2): i=3>j=2, return 0. So count(2,3)=1+1-0=2. count(1,2): s[1]=b, s[2]=c, not equal: count(1,2) = count(2,2) + count(1,1) - count(2,1). count(2,2)=1, count(1,1)=1, count(2,1): i=2>j=1, return 0. So count(1,2)=1+1-0=2. count(2,2)=1. Thus count(1,3)=2+2-1=3. Now count(0,2): s[0]=a, s[2]=c, not equal: count(0,2) = count(1,2) + count(0,1) - count(1,1). count(1,2)=2, count(0,1): s[0]=a, s[1]=b, not equal: count(0,1) = count(1,1) + count(0,0) - count(1,0). count(1,1)=1, count(0,0)=1, count(1,0): i=1>j=0, return 0. So count(0,1)=1+1-0=2. count(1,1)=1. Thus count(0,2)=2+2-1=3. count(1,2)=2. So count(0,3)=3+3-2=4. Palindromic subsequences: a, b, c, d (all single characters).

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Function with Matching Character Handling
ladders.desc: Implement the recursive function that counts palindromic subsequences for a substring, handling cases where start and end characters match or differ.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the DP approach: a function count(i, j) that computes the number of distinct palindromic subsequences in the substring s[i:j+1]. The function uses recursion with memoization to avoid redundant calculations. The base cases: if i > j, return 0 (empty substring has no palindromes); if i == j, return 1 (single character is a palindrome). For the recursive case, we check if s[i] == s[j]. If they differ, the count is count(i+1, j) + count(i, j-1) - count(i+1, j-1), representing palindromes that include s[i], include s[j], or exclude both, with the overlap subtracted. If they match, we need to handle duplicates carefully. Find the next occurrence of s[i] from the left within (i, j) exclusive, call it low, and from the right within (i, j) exclusive, call it high. If low > high (no inner occurrence), then count(i, j) = 2 * count(i+1, j-1) + 2, accounting for the single-character palindrome s[i] and the two-character palindrome s[i]s[j] added to twice the inner count. If low == high (exactly one inner occurrence), count(i, j) = 2 * count(i+1, j-1) + 1, adding only the two-character palindrome. If low < high (multiple inner occurrences), count(i, j) = 2 * count(i+1, j-1) - count(low+1, high-1), subtracting the inner count to avoid overcounting duplicates. The intuition is that when characters match, we can form palindromes by wrapping the inner subsequences with the matching characters, but we must adjust for duplicates that arise from multiple inner occurrences of the same character. This sub-routine connects to L0 as it is called recursively to solve the full problem. Time complexity for this function with memoization is O(n²) overall, but each call is O(1) except for finding low and high, which can be precomputed. Space is O(n²) for the memo table. Common mistakes include incorrect indices in the recursive calls, mishandling the matching case, or not using modulo arithmetic for large results. The function should return results modulo 10^9+7 as typical in such problems.

examples.trace: Call count(0,3): s[0]=b, s[3]=b match. Find low: next b after index 0 within (0,3): check indices 1 and 2, s[1]=c, s[2]=c, no b, so low = infinity (or not found). high: previous b before index 3 within (0,3): indices 2 and 1, no b, so high = -infinity. Thus low > high. Compute count(1,2): s[1]=c, s[2]=c match. Find low: next c after index 1 within (1,2): no indices, low > high. Compute count(2,1): i=2, j=1, i>j, return 0. So count(1,2) = 2*0 + 2 = 2. Then count(0,3) = 2*2 + 2 = 6.

Call count(1,2): s[1]=b, s[2]=a, not equal. So count(1,2) = count(2,2) + count(1,1) - count(2,1). count(2,2)=1, count(1,1)=1, count(2,1): i=2>j=1, return 0. Thus count(1,2)=1+1-0=2. Palindromes in "ba": b and a.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Handling Matching Characters to Avoid Duplicates
ladders.desc: The central technique for counting distinct palindromes when start and end characters are the same, preventing overcounting.
ladders.explanation: When the characters at the start and end of a substring are the same (s[i] == s[j]), we can form new palindromic subsequences by taking any palindrome from the inner substring s[i+1:j] and wrapping it with s[i] and s[j]. However, this can lead to duplicates if the inner substring contains multiple occurrences of the same character. The core logic addresses this by finding the next occurrences of s[i] from the left (low) and right (high) within the range (i, j) exclusive. There are three cases: 1) If low > high, meaning no inner occurrence of s[i] exists, then all palindromes formed by wrapping are distinct. We add 2 for the single-character palindrome s[i] and the two-character palindrome s[i]s[j], and double the count from the inner substring: count(i, j) = 2 * count(i+1, j-1) + 2. 2) If low == high, exactly one inner occurrence exists. Here, the two-character palindrome s[i]s[j] is new, but the single-character palindrome s[i] is already counted in the inner substring (as s[low] is the same character). So we add 1: count(i, j) = 2 * count(i+1, j-1) + 1. 3) If low < high, multiple inner occurrences exist. In this case, wrapping the inner substring can produce duplicates because the same palindrome might be formed using different inner occurrences. To avoid this, we subtract the count of palindromes in the substring between low and high exclusive: count(i, j) = 2 * count(i+1, j-1) - count(low+1, high-1). The intuition is that the inner count between low and high represents palindromes that would be counted twice when doubling. This logic ensures each distinct palindrome is counted exactly once. It connects to L1 as it is applied within the recursive function. Time complexity for this logic is O(n) if we search for low and high linearly, but precomputation can make it O(1). Space is O(1) for the variables. Common mistakes include incorrect handling of the cases, especially when low and high are not found, or mis-indexing the inner substring. This technique is crucial for the DP solution's correctness.

examples.trace: s[0]=b, s[3]=b match. Find low: next b after index 0 within (0,3): indices 1 and 2 are 'c','c', no b, so low not found (treat as inf). high: previous b before index 3 within (0,3): indices 2 and 1, no b, so high not found (treat as -inf). Thus low > high. Inner count count(1,2)=2. Then count(0,3)=2*2+2=6.

s[0]=a, s[2]=a match. Find low: next a after index 0 within (0,2): index 1 is 'b', no a, so low not found? Wait, within (0,2) exclusive means index 1 only, which is 'b', so low not found (inf). high: previous a before index 2 within (0,2): index 1 is 'b', no a, so high not found (-inf). Thus low > high? But in "aba", there is no inner 'a', so it should be low > high. Let's compute: count(1,1)=1. Then count(0,2)=2*1+2=4. Actually, for "aba", palindromes: a, b, aa, aba. That's 4. So case is low > high, not low == high. Correcting: low > high because no inner 'a'. So output should be case low > high, count=4.

ladders.title: Building Block — Recursive DP for Substring Palindromes
ladders.desc: Using recursion with memoization to count palindromic subsequences in a substring, without handling duplicates.
ladders.explanation: This building block focuses on the recursive dynamic programming approach to count all palindromic subsequences in a substring s[i:j+1], but initially without worrying about distinct counts or handling matching characters specially. We define a function count(i, j) that returns the number of palindromic subsequences. The base cases: if i > j, return 0; if i == j, return 1. For the recursive case, we consider whether s[i] and s[j] are equal. If they are equal, then every palindrome in the inner substring s[i+1:j] can be extended by adding s[i] and s[j] to form a new palindrome. Additionally, we have the single-character palindrome s[i] and the two-character palindrome s[i]s[j]. So, count(i, j) = count(i+1, j-1) + count(i+1, j-1) + 1 + 1? Actually, to avoid overcomplication, we use a simpler formula: count(i, j) = count(i+1, j) + count(i, j-1) - count(i+1, j-1) + (s[i] == s[j] ? count(i+1, j-1) + 1 : 0). But wait, that might not be correct for distinct counts. For this building block, we'll use a basic recurrence: if s[i] == s[j], count(i, j) = count(i+1, j) + count(i, j-1) + 1; else, count(i, j) = count(i+1, j) + count(i, j-1) - count(i+1, j-1). This counts all subsequences, including duplicates. We use memoization to store results in a 2D table to avoid recomputation. The intuition is breaking the problem into smaller subproblems by including or excluding characters at the ends. This connects to L2 as it provides the foundation, and L2 adds the logic to handle duplicates. Time complexity is O(n²) due to n² subproblems, each solved in O(1) with memoization. Space complexity is O(n²) for the memo table. Common mistakes include incorrect recurrence relations, off-by-one errors in indices, or not handling base cases properly. This block helps understand the recursive structure before adding the complexity of distinct counts.

examples.trace: count(0,1): s[0]=a, s[1]=b, not equal. So count(0,1) = count(1,1) + count(0,0) - count(1,0). count(1,1)=1, count(0,0)=1, count(1,0): i=1>j=0, return 0. Thus count(0,1)=1+1-0=2? Wait, the formula for not equal should be count(i+1,j) + count(i,j-1) - count(i+1,j-1). Here, count(1,1)=1, count(0,0)=1, count(1,0)=0, so 1+1-0=2. But palindromic subsequences in "ab": a and b, so 2. Actually, the output should be 2, not 3. Correcting: for this building block, we're counting all, including duplicates? In "ab", no duplicates, so 2. So input should yield 2. Let's use a different example.

count(0,1): s[0]=a, s[1]=a, equal. So count(0,1) = count(1,1) + count(0,0) + 1. count(1,1)=1, count(0,0)=1, so 1+1+1=3. Palindromic subsequences in "aa": a, a, aa. But note: the two 'a's are from different indices, so if we count all subsequences, we have a (first), a (second), and aa. That's 3. This includes duplicates if we consider positions, but for distinct counts, it would be 2. This building block counts all, so 3 is correct.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Checking for Palindromic Subsequences in a Substring
ladders.desc: Determining if a given subsequence is palindromic and counting simple cases.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves checking whether a subsequence of a string is palindromic. A subsequence is palindromic if it reads the same forwards and backwards. For example, in string "aba", the subsequence "aa" (from indices 0 and 2) is palindromic. To check, we can extract the subsequence and compare it to its reverse. However, in the context of counting, we often need to consider all subsequences. A simpler task is to count palindromic subsequences in trivial cases: for a single character, there is exactly one palindrome (the character itself). For two characters, if they are the same, there are three palindromic subsequences: the first character, the second character, and the two-character sequence. If they are different, there are two: each character individually. This operation uses basic string manipulation and comparison. The intuition is that palindromes are symmetric, so we can verify by checking characters from both ends. This connects to L3 as it is the foundational check used in the recursive DP. Time complexity for checking a subsequence is O(k) where k is the length of the subsequence. Space is O(k) for storing the subsequence. Common mistakes include confusing subsequences with substrings, or not considering all possible subsequences. This operation is essential for understanding what we are counting in the larger problem.

examples.trace: Subsequence from indices 0 and 2: characters are s[0]='a' and s[2]='a', so the subsequence is "aa". Reverse of "aa" is "aa", which is the same, so it is palindromic.
Subsequence from indices 0,1,2: characters are 'a','b','c', so "abc". Reverse is "cba", which is different, so not palindromic.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Subsequences and Palindromes
ladders.desc: Basic definitions and examples of subsequences and palindromes.
ladders.explanation: This foundation covers the fundamental concepts needed for the problem: subsequences and palindromes. A subsequence of a string is a sequence that can be derived by deleting zero or more characters without changing the order of the remaining characters. For example, for the string "abc", some subsequences are "a", "b", "c", "ab", "ac", "bc", "abc", and the empty string. Note that "ba" is not a subsequence because the order is changed. A palindrome is a string that reads the same forwards and backwards, such as "racecar" or "aba". In this problem, we are interested in palindromic subsequences: subsequences that are also palindromes. For instance, in "aba", the subsequence "aa" (from indices 0 and 2) is palindromic. The key intuition is that we need to count all such subsequences that are distinct, meaning we count each unique palindrome only once, even if it can be formed from different sets of indices. This concept is prerequisite because the entire problem revolves around generating and counting these objects. It connects to L4 as we use this understanding to check for palindromes. Common mistakes include confusing subsequences with substrings (which are contiguous) or not considering the empty subsequence (which is typically not counted as a palindrome in this problem). Beginners should practice listing all subsequences of small strings and identifying which are palindromes to build intuition.

examples.trace: List all subsequences: start with empty string '', then take characters: 'a', 'b', and combine: 'ab'. Check each: '' is empty, not typically counted as palindrome; 'a' reads same forwards and backwards, so palindrome; 'b' palindrome; 'ab' reverse is 'ba', not palindrome. So palindromic subsequences are 'a' and 'b'.

Subsequences: empty '', first 'a', second 'a', and 'aa'. Note: the two 'a's are from different indices, so they are distinct subsequences but same string 'a'. For palindromes: 'a' (first) palindrome, 'a' (second) palindrome, 'aa' palindrome. So three palindromic subsequences, but if counting distinct, only 'a' and 'aa'.

testCases.expected: Subsequences: many; Palindromic subsequences: 'r', 'a', 'c', 'e', 'cec'? No, 'cec' not subsequence; list: 'r','a','c','e'

approaches.name: DP Iterative Bottom-Up O(n^2)
approaches.summary: Build a DP table iteratively where dp[i][j] counts palindromic subsequences in substring s[i:j], using recurrence relations based on character matches. This avoids recursion overhead and fills the table in a systematic order.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Different Palindromic Subsequences with DP
ladders.desc: Complete solution using iterative bottom-up DP to count distinct palindromic subsequences in a string.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires counting all distinct palindromic subsequences in a given string s of length n. A subsequence is a sequence derived by deleting some characters without changing the order. A palindrome reads the same forwards and backwards. The challenge is to avoid counting duplicates. The DP approach uses a 2D table dp[i][j] representing the count of distinct palindromic subsequences in substring s[i:j+1]. The recurrence relation depends on whether s[i] equals s[j]. If they differ, dp[i][j] = dp[i+1][j] + dp[i][j-1] - dp[i+1][j-1] (inclusion-exclusion). If they match, we must consider inner subsequences and avoid overcounting. Specifically, let left = i+1, right = j-1, and find the first occurrence of s[i] from left to right (next_left) and last occurrence from right to left (prev_right). If next_left > prev_right, no same character inside: dp[i][j] = 2*dp[i+1][j-1] + 2 (adding s[i] alone and s[i]s[i] as new palindromes). If next_left == prev_right, exactly one same character inside: dp[i][j] = 2*dp[i+1][j-1] + 1 (adding s[i] alone). If next_left < prev_right, multiple same characters inside: dp[i][j] = 2*dp[i+1][j-1] - dp[next_left+1][prev_right-1] (subtract inner duplicates). Base cases: dp[i][i] = 1 (single character), and empty substrings (i>j) = 0. Fill table for increasing substring lengths. Time O(n^2) as we fill n^2 states with O(1) work each. Space O(n^2) for the DP table. Connection to L1: This integrates the recurrence logic with boundary handling. Common mistakes: Forgetting modulo (if required), mishandling duplicate counting in equal character case, incorrect base cases. Real code pattern: Initialize n x n DP with zeros, set diagonals to 1, loop length from 2 to n, for i from 0 to n-length, j = i+length-1, apply recurrence with character checks.

examples.trace: String: bccb, n=4. DP table 4x4. Initialize dp[i][i]=1: dp[0][0]=1 (b), dp[1][1]=1 (c), dp[2][2]=1 (c), dp[3][3]=1 (b). Length 2: i=0,j=1: s[0]=b, s[1]=c, different, dp[0][1]=dp[1][1]+dp[0][0]-dp[1][0]=1+1-0=2 (subseq: b,c). i=1,j=2: s[1]=c, s[2]=c, equal, left=2, right=1, next_left=2, prev_right=1, next_left>prev_right, dp[1][2]=2*dp[2][1]+2=2*0+2=2 (subseq: c,cc). i=2,j=3: s[2]=c, s[3]=b, different, dp[2][3]=dp[3][3]+dp[2][2]-dp[3][2]=1+1-0=2 (subseq: c,b). Length 3: i=0,j=2: s[0]=b, s[2]=c, different, dp[0][2]=dp[1][2]+dp[0][1]-dp[1][1]=2+2-1=3 (subseq: b,c,cc,bc? Wait, distinct: b,c,cc,bc,cb? Let's compute: from dp[1][2]={c,cc}, dp[0][1]={b,c}, intersection dp[1][1]={c}, so {c,cc} U {b,c} = {b,c,cc}, minus {c} gives {b,c,cc}. Actually bc and cb are not palindromic, so correct. i=1,j=3: s[1]=c, s[3]=b, different, dp[1][3]=dp[2][3]+dp[1][2]-dp[2][2]=2+2-1=3 (subseq: c,b,cc). Length 4: i=0,j=3: s[0]=b, s[3]=b, equal, left=1, right=2, find next_left: first b from index 1 to 2? None, so next_left=inf, prev_right: last b from 2 to 1? None, so prev_right=-inf, next_left>prev_right, dp[0][3]=2*dp[1][2]+2=2*2+2=6. Subseq: from inner dp[1][2]={c,cc}, double: {c,cc} and b{c,cc}b gives {bcb,bccb}, plus b and bb, total: c,cc,bcb,bccb,b,bb = 6. Output dp[0][3]=6.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recurrence for Equal Characters in DP
ladders.desc: Handle the case when s[i] equals s[j] in the DP recurrence to count distinct palindromic subsequences.
ladders.explanation: In the DP table dp[i][j] for substring s[i:j+1], when s[i] equals s[j], we cannot simply use inclusion-exclusion because adding s[i] and s[j] creates new palindromes and may duplicate existing ones. The intuition: inner subsequences in dp[i+1][j-1] can be extended by wrapping s[i] and s[j] around them, but if the same character appears inside, duplicates arise. Steps: 1. Compute left = i+1, right = j-1. 2. Find next_left = smallest index in [left, right] where s[next_left] == s[i], or inf if none. 3. Find prev_right = largest index in [right, left] where s[prev_right] == s[i], or -inf if none. 4. Three cases: a) If next_left > prev_right: no same character inside, all inner subsequences are distinct when wrapped. dp[i][j] = 2*dp[i+1][j-1] + 2. The +2 adds s[i] alone and s[i]s[i] (i.e., the double character). b) If next_left == prev_right: exactly one same character inside, s[i] alone is already counted in inner dp (as that character), so dp[i][j] = 2*dp[i+1][j-1] + 1. c) If next_left < prev_right: multiple same characters inside, wrapping duplicates inner subsequences that are between next_left and prev_right. dp[i][j] = 2*dp[i+1][j-1] - dp[next_left+1][prev_right-1]. Time O(n) per dp[i][j] if scanning for next_left/prev_right, but can be preprocessed to O(1). Space O(1) extra. Connection to L0: This is the core of the DP recurrence. Common mistakes: Off-by-one in indices, misunderstanding the three cases, not handling empty inner ranges. Real code pattern: if s[i]==s[j]: left,right = i+1,j-1; find next_left and prev_right; apply case formula.

examples.trace: s[0]=b, s[3]=b, equal. left=1, right=2. Find next_left: scan from index 1 to 2 for 'b': s[1]=c, s[2]=c, none, so next_left=inf. Find prev_right: scan from 2 to 1 for 'b': none, prev_right=-inf. next_left>prev_right, case a. dp[i+1][j-1]=dp[1][2]=2 (subseq: c,cc). dp[0][3]=2*2+2=6. As in L0.

s[0]=a, s[2]=a, equal. left=1, right=1. Find next_left: index 1, s[1]=b != a, so none, next_left=inf. prev_right: index 1, !=a, prev_right=-inf. next_left>prev_right, case a. dp[i+1][j-1]=dp[1][1]=1 (subseq: b). dp[0][2]=2*1+2=4. Subseq: from inner {b}, wrap gives {aba}, plus a and aa, total: a,b,aa,aba=4.

ladders.title: Core Logic — DP Recurrence for Palindromic Subsequences
ladders.desc: Define the recurrence relation for dp[i][j] based on character matches.
ladders.explanation: The DP table dp[i][j] counts distinct palindromic subsequences in substring s[i:j+1]. The recurrence splits into two cases based on whether s[i] equals s[j]. Intuition: Any palindromic subsequence in s[i:j+1] either includes both s[i] and s[j], includes only one, or includes neither. If s[i] != s[j], they cannot both be in the same palindrome unless equal, so we consider subsequences from dp[i+1][j] (excluding s[i]), dp[i][j-1] (excluding s[j]), and subtract dp[i+1][j-1] (intersection counted twice). If s[i] == s[j], they can be included together, wrapping around inner subsequences. The recurrence: if s[i] != s[j]: dp[i][j] = dp[i+1][j] + dp[i][j-1] - dp[i+1][j-1]. If s[i] == s[j]: let inner = dp[i+1][j-1], then dp[i][j] = 2*inner + (cases for new palindromes as in L1). Base cases: dp[i][i] = 1 for single character, dp[i][j] = 0 for i>j. Fill order: increasing substring length from 1 to n. Time O(n^2) for filling table, each cell O(1) if equal case handled efficiently. Space O(n^2). Connection to L1: This general recurrence uses the equal-character handling as a subroutine. Common mistakes: Incorrect subtraction in unequal case, not handling base cases, filling order errors. Real code pattern: for length in 1 to n: for i in 0 to n-length: j=i+length-1; if s[i]==s[j]: compute as in L1 else dp[i][j]=dp[i+1][j]+dp[i][j-1]-dp[i+1][j-1].

examples.trace: s[0]=b, s[2]=b, equal. Use L1: left=1, right=1, s[1]=c, no b inside, next_left>prev_right, case a. dp[i+1][j-1]=dp[1][1]=1 (c). dp[0][2]=2*1+2=4. Subseq: b,c,bb,bcb.

ladders.title: Building Block — Counting Subsequences with Inclusion-Exclusion
ladders.desc: Use inclusion-exclusion principle to count distinct subsequences in overlapping subproblems.
ladders.explanation: When computing counts over sets, if we have two overlapping sets A and B, the count of elements in A ∪ B is |A| + |B| - |A ∩ B|. In DP for subsequences, dp[i][j] for s[i] != s[j] uses this: A = subsequences in s[i+1:j+1] (dp[i+1][j]), B = subsequences in s[i:j] (dp[i][j-1]), and A ∩ B = subsequences in s[i+1:j] (dp[i+1][j-1]). This avoids double-counting subsequences that appear in both A and B. Intuition: Any palindromic subsequence in s[i:j+1] either does not include s[i] (so in A), or does not include s[j] (so in B), but those excluding both are in both A and B. Steps: 1. Compute count_A = dp[i+1][j]. 2. Compute count_B = dp[i][j-1]. 3. Compute count_intersection = dp[i+1][j-1]. 4. dp[i][j] = count_A + count_B - count_intersection. Ensure base cases: for empty substring (i>j), count is 0. Time O(1) per application. Space O(1). Connection to L2: This is the unequal-character case in the recurrence. Common mistakes: Subtracting wrong intersection, not initializing base cases, misunderstanding set definitions. Real code pattern: if s[i]!=s[j]: dp[i][j] = dp[i+1][j] + dp[i][j-1] - dp[i+1][j-1].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterate Over All Substrings in DP
ladders.desc: Fill a 2D DP table by iterating over all substrings in increasing length order.
ladders.explanation: Dynamic programming often requires computing values for all subproblems in a systematic order. For a string s of length n, define dp[i][j] for 0 ≤ i ≤ j < n. To ensure that when computing dp[i][j], the required subproblems (e.g., dp[i+1][j], dp[i][j-1], dp[i+1][j-1]) are already computed, we iterate by increasing substring length. Intuition: Smaller substrings are building blocks for larger ones. Steps: 1. Initialize dp as n x n matrix with zeros. 2. Set base cases: for i from 0 to n-1, dp[i][i] = 1 (single character substring). 3. For length from 2 to n (inclusive): for i from 0 to n-length: j = i + length - 1; compute dp[i][j] using recurrence based on s[i] and s[j]. This order guarantees that for any dp[i][j], the subproblems dp[i+1][j], dp[i][j-1], dp[i+1][j-1] have been computed because they correspond to smaller lengths (j-i < length). Time O(n^2) as double loop over lengths and start indices. Space O(n^2) for the table. Connection to L3: This iteration framework applies the inclusion-exclusion or other recurrences. Common mistakes: Incorrect loop bounds (off-by-one), not initializing diagonals, using wrong length range. Real code pattern: n=len(s); dp=[[0]*n for _ in range(n)]; for i in range(n): dp[i][i]=1; for L in range(2,n+1): for i in range(n-L+1): j=i+L-1; compute dp[i][j].

examples.trace: n=3. Initialize dp[0][0]=1, dp[1][1]=1, dp[2][2]=1. Length 2: i=0,j=1: compute dp[0][1]. i=1,j=2: compute dp[1][2]. Length 3: i=0,j=2: compute dp[0][2]. Table filled.
n=2. Initialize dp[0][0]=1, dp[1][1]=1. Length 2: i=0,j=1: compute dp[0][1]. Done.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Palindromic Subsequence Definition
ladders.desc: Understand what a palindromic subsequence is and how it differs from substrings.
ladders.explanation: A subsequence of a string is a sequence that can be derived by deleting zero or more characters without changing the order of the remaining characters. For example, in string 'abc', subsequences include '', 'a', 'b', 'c', 'ab', 'ac', 'bc', 'abc'. A palindrome is a string that reads the same forwards and backwards, like 'racecar' or 'aa'. A palindromic subsequence is a subsequence that is also a palindrome. For example, in 'abca', palindromic subsequences include 'a', 'b', 'c', 'aa', 'aca'. Note that 'aa' is a subsequence (take first and last 'a'), not necessarily contiguous. Key differences from substring: substring must be contiguous, subsequence need not be. Intuition: Counting distinct palindromic subsequences requires considering all possible selections of characters in order that form palindromes, and ensuring no duplicates. Steps to identify: 1. Given string s, list all subsequences (exponential, but conceptually). 2. Check each if it is a palindrome (compare characters from ends inward). 3. Collect distinct ones. Time O(2^n * n) for brute force, hence need DP. Space O(1) for concept. Connection to L4: This foundational concept motivates the DP problem. Common mistakes: Confusing subsequence with substring, forgetting empty subsequence (usually not counted as palindrome unless specified), not considering distinctness. Real code pattern: is_palindrome(sub): return sub == sub[::-1].

examples.trace: All subsequences: '', a, b, c, a (last a), ab, ac, aa, bc, ba, ca, ca, abc, aba, aca, bca, abca. Check palindromes: '' (not typically counted), a (yes), b (yes), c (yes), a (yes, duplicate), ab (no), ac (no), aa (yes), bc (no), ba (no), ca (no), ca (no), abc (no), aba (no, not in order? Actually 'aba' is not a subsequence of 'abca' because order would be a,b,a but last a is after c, so invalid), aca (yes, take a,c,a), bca (no), abca (no). Distinct: a,b,c,aa,aca. So 5.
Subsequences: '', a, a, aa. Palindromes: '' (not counted), a (yes), a (duplicate), aa (yes). Distinct: a,aa. So 2.

approaches.name: DP with Optimized Space O(n^2)
approaches.summary: Modify the iterative DP approach to use only O(n) space by maintaining two rows or a rolling array, while still counting distinct palindromic subsequences accurately. Reduces memory usage without sacrificing correctness.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Distinct Palindromic Subsequences with O(n) Space DP
ladders.desc: Complete solution using optimized DP with rolling array to count distinct palindromic subsequences in O(n²) time and O(n) space.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires counting all distinct palindromic subsequences in a given string. A subsequence is a sequence derived by deleting some characters without changing order. A palindrome reads the same forwards and backwards. Distinct means we count each unique subsequence only once, even if it appears multiple times. The optimized DP approach uses dynamic programming with a rolling array to reduce space from O(n²) to O(n) while maintaining correctness. The intuition is to build solutions for substrings and use previous results to avoid recomputation. We define dp[i][j] as the count of distinct palindromic subsequences in substring s[i:j+1]. The recurrence: if s[i] != s[j], dp[i][j] = dp[i+1][j] + dp[i][j-1] - dp[i+1][j-1] (inclusion-exclusion). If s[i] == s[j], we find the next occurrence of s[i] from left (next) and previous from right (prev) within (i,j). If next > prev, no same character inside, dp[i][j] = 2*dp[i+1][j-1] + 2. If next == prev, exactly one same character inside, dp[i][j] = 2*dp[i+1][j-1] + 1. If next < prev, at least two same characters inside, dp[i][j] = 2*dp[i+1][j-1] - dp[next+1][prev-1]. We use modulo to handle large numbers. To optimize space, we maintain only two rows (current and previous) since dp[i][j] depends on dp[i+1][*] and dp[i][*]. We iterate i from n-1 down to 0 and j from i to n-1, updating a 1D array dp_curr[j] based on dp_prev[j] (which stores dp[i+1][j]) and previously computed dp_curr[k] for k<j. We also precompute next and prev arrays for each character and position to handle the equal character case efficiently in O(1). Time complexity is O(n²) for filling DP table and O(n²) for precomputation, overall O(n²). Space is O(n) for the rolling arrays and O(n) for precomputation arrays, overall O(n). Connection to L1: This integrates the key subroutine of handling equal characters with next/prev logic. Common mistakes: forgetting to handle distinct counts properly with next/prev, incorrect modulo operations, or space optimization errors. Real code pattern: Initialize dp_prev and dp_curr arrays of size n. Precompute next and prev arrays. Loop i from n-1 to 0, for j from i to n-1, compute based on recurrence, update dp_curr[j], then copy dp_curr to dp_prev for next i. Return dp_curr[n-1].

examples.trace: String: bccb, n=4. Precompute next and prev (simplified). Initialize dp_prev=[0]*4, dp_curr=[0]*4. i=3: j=3: s[3]=b, dp_curr[3]=1 (single char). dp_prev=dp_curr=[0,0,0,1]. i=2: j=2: s[2]=c, dp_curr[2]=1. j=3: s[2]!=s[3] (c!=b), dp_curr[3]=dp_prev[3]+dp_curr[2]-dp_prev[2]=1+1-0=2. dp_prev=[0,0,1,2]. i=1: j=1: s[1]=c, dp_curr[1]=1. j=2: s[1]==s[2] (c==c), next=2, prev=1, next==prev, dp_curr[2]=2*dp_prev[1]+1=2*1+1=3. j=3: s[1]!=s[3] (c!=b), dp_curr[3]=dp_prev[3]+dp_curr[2]-dp_prev[2]=2+3-1=4. dp_prev=[0,1,3,4]. i=0: j=0: s[0]=b, dp_curr[0]=1. j=1: s[0]!=s[1] (b!=c), dp_curr[1]=dp_prev[1]+dp_curr[0]-dp_prev[0]=1+1-0=2. j=2: s[0]!=s[2] (b!=c), dp_curr[2]=dp_prev[2]+dp_curr[1]-dp_prev[1]=3+2-1=4. j=3: s[0]==s[3] (b==b), next=3, prev=0, next>prev, dp_curr[3]=2*dp_prev[2]+2=2*3+2=8. But distinct count: subsequences: 'b','c','bb','cc','bcb','bccb' =6. Our DP counts all, including duplicates? Actually recurrence handles distinct. Let's recompute carefully: For s[0]==s[3], next=3, prev=0, next>prev means no same char inside, so dp=2*dp[i+1][j-1]+2=2*dp[1][2]+2. dp[1][2] from earlier: for substring s[1:3]='cc', distinct palindromic subsequences: 'c','cc'=2. So 2*2+2=6. Yes, dp_curr[3]=6. Final output 6.

String: abcd, n=4. All characters distinct. Palindromic subsequences: each single character 'a','b','c','d' =4. Initialize dp arrays. i=3: j=3: dp_curr[3]=1. dp_prev=[0,0,0,1]. i=2: j=2: dp_curr[2]=1, j=3: s[2]!=s[3], dp_curr[3]=dp_prev[3]+dp_curr[2]-dp_prev[2]=1+1-0=2. dp_prev=[0,0,1,2]. i=1: j=1: dp_curr[1]=1, j=2: s[1]!=s[2], dp_curr[2]=dp_prev[2]+dp_curr[1]-dp_prev[1]=1+1-0=2, j=3: s[1]!=s[3], dp_curr[3]=dp_prev[3]+dp_curr[2]-dp_prev[2]=2+2-1=3. dp_prev=[0,1,2,3]. i=0: j=0: dp_curr[0]=1, j=1: s[0]!=s[1], dp_curr[1]=dp_prev[1]+dp_curr[0]-dp_prev[0]=1+1-0=2, j=2: s[0]!=s[2], dp_curr[2]=dp_prev[2]+dp_curr[1]-dp_prev[1]=2+2-1=3, j=3: s[0]!=s[3], dp_curr[3]=dp_prev[3]+dp_curr[2]-dp_prev[2]=3+3-2=4. Final output 4.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Handling Equal Characters with Next/Prev Indices in DP
ladders.desc: Compute DP recurrence when s[i]==s[j] using next and previous occurrence indices to count distinct subsequences.
ladders.explanation: In the DP for counting distinct palindromic subsequences, when the endpoints s[i] and s[j] are equal, we need to carefully count to avoid duplicates. The recurrence uses next and prev indices: next is the smallest index > i such that s[next]==s[i] and next<=j, prev is the largest index < j such that s[prev]==s[j] and prev>=i. These help determine how many same characters are inside the substring (i,j). The intuition: when we add s[i] and s[j] to palindromic subsequences of substring (i+1,j-1), we might create duplicates if there are same characters inside that can form the same subsequences. Cases: 1) If next > prev, no same character inside, all new subsequences are distinct: we take all from dp[i+1][j-1], add s[i] alone, add s[j] alone, and add s[i]+s[j] pair, so total = 2*dp[i+1][j-1] + 2. 2) If next == prev, exactly one same character inside, then s[i] alone and s[j] alone are same as that inner character, so we avoid double-counting: total = 2*dp[i+1][j-1] + 1. 3) If next < prev, at least two same characters inside, then some subsequences from dp[i+1][j-1] when wrapped with s[i] and s[j] might duplicate those from inner same characters, so we subtract dp[next+1][prev-1] to remove duplicates: total = 2*dp[i+1][j-1] - dp[next+1][prev-1]. This ensures distinct counts. We precompute next and prev arrays for each position and character to get these indices in O(1) time. Time complexity for this subroutine is O(1) per DP cell, but precomputation is O(n²) or O(n*alphabet). Space for precomputation is O(n*alphabet). Connection to L0: This is the core of the DP recurrence, integrated into the full solution. Common mistakes: incorrect handling of indices, off-by-one errors, or misunderstanding the cases. Real code pattern: Precompute next[i][c] for each i and character c (e.g., 'a'-'d') as next occurrence index, similarly prev[i][c]. In DP loop, if s[i]==s[j], let c=s[i], next_idx=next[i][c], prev_idx=prev[j][c]. Adjust if indices out of range. Then apply case logic.

examples.trace: String s='aba', i=0 (s[0]='a'), j=2 (s[2]='a'), they are equal. Substring (i+1,j-1) is s[1]='b'. dp[i+1][j-1]=dp[1][1]=1 (single char 'b'). Find next: smallest index >0 with 'a', next=2. Find prev: largest index <2 with 'a', prev=0. Here next=2, prev=0, next>prev, so case 1: dp[0][2]=2*dp[1][1]+2=2*1+2=4. Distinct palindromic subsequences in 'aba': 'a','b','aa','aba' =4.

String s='aaa', i=0 (s[0]='a'), j=2 (s[2]='a'), equal. Substring (i+1,j-1)='a', dp[1][1]=1. Find next: index>0 with 'a', next=1. Find prev: index<2 with 'a', prev=1. next=1, prev=1, next==prev, case 2: dp[0][2]=2*dp[1][1]+1=2*1+1=3. Distinct palindromic subsequences in 'aaa': 'a','aa','aaa' =3.

ladders.title: Core Logic — DP Recurrence for Distinct Palindromic Subsequences
ladders.desc: Derive and apply the DP recurrence relation to count distinct palindromic subsequences in substrings.
ladders.explanation: The core logic uses dynamic programming to build solutions for substrings based on smaller substrings. Define dp[i][j] as the number of distinct palindromic subsequences in substring s[i:j+1]. Base case: dp[i][i]=1 for single character. For i<j, we consider cases based on s[i] and s[j]. If s[i] != s[j], then palindromic subsequences can be those entirely within s[i+1:j+1] or s[i:j], but we must subtract overlap (those in s[i+1:j]) to avoid double-counting: dp[i][j] = dp[i+1][j] + dp[i][j-1] - dp[i+1][j-1]. If s[i] == s[j], let x = s[i]. We need to count subsequences that include both endpoints. Consider all palindromic subsequences in s[i+1:j]. Adding x to both ends of each gives new palindromes. Also, x alone and xx are new. But if there are same characters inside, duplicates may occur. To handle distinct counts, find next occurrence of x after i (next) and previous occurrence before j (prev). Cases: 1) No x inside (next>prev): all new are distinct, dp[i][j] = 2*dp[i+1][j-1] + 2. 2) One x inside (next==prev): x alone duplicates the inner x, so dp[i][j] = 2*dp[i+1][j-1] + 1. 3) At least two x inside (next<prev): some duplicates from inner x's, subtract dp[next+1][prev-1]: dp[i][j] = 2*dp[i+1][j-1] - dp[next+1][prev-1]. This recurrence ensures we count each distinct subsequence exactly once. The intuition is combinatorial inclusion-exclusion with careful handling of identical characters. Time complexity per DP cell is O(1) with precomputed next/prev, overall O(n²). Space for DP table is O(n²) in standard form. Connection to L1: This recurrence is implemented using the next/prev subroutine. Common mistakes: forgetting to handle distinct counts, incorrect subtraction, or base case errors. Real code pattern: Initialize dp as 2D array. Loop i from n-1 down to 0, for j from i to n-1, compute based on s[i] and s[j].

examples.trace: s='ab', i=0, j=1. s[0]='a', s[1]='b', not equal. dp[i][j] = dp[i+1][j] + dp[i][j-1] - dp[i+1][j-1]. dp[1][1]=1 (single 'b'), dp[0][0]=1 (single 'a'), dp[1][0] is invalid (i>j), treat as 0. So dp[0][1]=1+1-0=2. Distinct palindromic subsequences: 'a','b' =2.

s='aa', i=0, j=1. s[0]=='a', s[1]=='a', equal. Find next: next occurrence of 'a' after i=0 is index 1. Prev: previous occurrence before j=1 is index 0. next=1, prev=0, next>prev, case 1: dp[i][j]=2*dp[i+1][j-1]+2. dp[i+1][j-1]=dp[1][0], invalid, treat as 0. So dp[0][1]=2*0+2=2. Distinct subsequences: 'a','aa' =2.

ladders.title: Building Block — Precomputing Next and Previous Occurrence Indices
ladders.desc: Compute arrays to quickly find next and previous occurrences of each character for any substring.
ladders.explanation: To efficiently handle the DP recurrence when s[i]==s[j], we need to find the next occurrence of that character after index i and previous occurrence before index j within the substring. Precomputing these indices allows O(1) lookup during DP. For each position i and each possible character c (e.g., 'a' to 'z'), we compute next[i][c] as the smallest index j such that j > i and s[j]==c, or n if no such index. Similarly, prev[i][c] as the largest index j such that j < i and s[j]==c, or -1 if none. This can be done with dynamic programming: iterate from left to right to fill next, and right to left for prev. For next: initialize next[n][c]=n for all c. For i from n-1 down to 0, for each c, if s[i]==c then next[i][c]=i else next[i][c]=next[i+1][c]. For prev: initialize prev[-1][c]=-1 (or use array starting at 0). For i from 0 to n-1, for each c, if s[i]==c then prev[i][c]=i else prev[i][c]=prev[i-1][c]. This takes O(n*alphabet) time and space. In practice, since alphabet is constant (e.g., 26), it's O(n). The intuition is to propagate the nearest occurrence index. During DP, for substring s[i:j+1], if s[i]==s[j]==c, then next occurrence within (i,j] is next[i][c] if <=j, else treat as beyond. Previous occurrence within [i,j) is prev[j][c] if >=i. This helps determine how many same characters are inside. Connection to L2: These indices are used in the DP recurrence for equal characters. Common mistakes: off-by-one in indices, not handling bounds properly, or inefficient computation. Real code pattern: Create 2D arrays next and prev of size (n+1)x26. Fill using loops.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Inclusion-Exclusion Principle for Substring Counts
ladders.desc: Apply inclusion-exclusion to combine counts from overlapping substrings without double-counting.
ladders.explanation: When counting palindromic subsequences in a substring s[i:j+1], if endpoints are different (s[i]!=s[j]), the total count is the sum of counts in s[i+1:j+1] and s[i:j], minus the count in s[i+1:j] to avoid double-counting subsequences that appear in both. This is an application of the inclusion-exclusion principle. Formally, let A be the set of palindromic subsequences in s[i+1:j+1], B be those in s[i:j]. Then palindromic subsequences in s[i:j+1] are those that either don't include s[i] (i.e., in A) or don't include s[j] (i.e., in B). But subsequences that don't include both s[i] and s[j] are in both A and B (specifically in s[i+1:j]), so we subtract that intersection. Thus, |A ∪ B| = |A| + |B| - |A ∩ B|. In DP terms, dp[i][j] = dp[i+1][j] + dp[i][j-1] - dp[i+1][j-1]. This ensures each subsequence is counted exactly once. The intuition is combinatorial: we partition based on inclusion of endpoints. Time complexity for this operation is O(1). Space is O(1) per computation. Connection to L3: This operation is used in the DP recurrence when characters are not equal. Common mistakes: forgetting to subtract the intersection, or misapplying when i+1>j-1 (handle as 0). Real code pattern: In DP loop, if s[i]!=s[j], compute val = dp[i+1][j] + dp[i][j-1] - (dp[i+1][j-1] if i+1<=j-1 else 0).

examples.trace: s='ab', i=0, j=1. s[0]='a', s[1]='b', not equal. dp[i+1][j] = dp[1][1] = 1 (subsequences in 'b': {'b'}). dp[i][j-1] = dp[0][0] = 1 (subsequences in 'a': {'a'}). dp[i+1][j-1] = dp[1][0], invalid since i+1=1, j-1=0, 1>0, so treat as 0. Then dp[0][1] = 1 + 1 - 0 = 2. This counts {'a','b'} correctly.

s='abc', i=0, j=2. s[0]='a', s[2]='c', not equal. dp[i+1][j] = dp[1][2]: subsequences in 'bc' are {'b','c'} =2. dp[i][j-1] = dp[0][1]: subsequences in 'ab' are {'a','b'} =2. dp[i+1][j-1] = dp[1][1]: subsequences in 'b' =1. Then dp[0][2] = 2 + 2 - 1 = 3. This counts {'a','b','c'} correctly for 'abc'.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Palindromic Subsequences and Basic Counting
ladders.desc: Understand what a palindromic subsequence is and count them in simple cases.
ladders.explanation: A subsequence of a string is a sequence that can be derived by deleting zero or more characters without changing the order of the remaining characters. For example, in string 'abc', subsequences include '', 'a', 'b', 'c', 'ab', 'ac', 'bc', 'abc'. A palindrome is a string that reads the same forwards and backwards, like 'a', 'aa', 'aba'. A palindromic subsequence is a subsequence that is also a palindrome. For example, in 'abc', the palindromic subsequences are 'a', 'b', 'c' (single characters). In 'aba', they are 'a', 'b', 'aa', 'aba'. We count distinct palindromic subsequences, meaning each unique subsequence is counted once, even if it can be formed in multiple ways. For instance, in 'aaa', the subsequence 'a' can be formed by taking any of the three 'a's, but we count it only once. So distinct palindromic subsequences in 'aaa' are 'a', 'aa', 'aaa' =3. Basic counting: for a string of length n, there are 2^n total subsequences (including empty), but we only want palindromic ones. For a single character string, there is 1 (the character itself). For two characters, if they are same, there are 2 ('a' and 'aa'); if different, there are 2 ('a' and 'b'). This foundation involves identifying palindromes and understanding subsequence generation. Time complexity to list all subsequences is exponential, but for counting we use DP. Space is O(1) for simple cases. Connection to L4: This basic counting is used in DP base cases and intuition. Common mistakes: confusing subsequence with substring, or not handling distinct counts. Real code pattern: For small strings, generate all subsequences, check if palindrome, add to set, count size.

examples.trace: String 'a' has length 1. Subsequences: '' and 'a'. Palindromic subsequences: 'a' (since '' is often not counted in this problem, or count as 0 if specified). So count =1.
String 'ab' has length 2. Subsequences: '', 'a', 'b', 'ab'. Palindromic subsequences: 'a' and 'b' (both single characters). 'ab' is not palindrome. So distinct count =2.
